metadata.title: Investment Portfolio Strategies
metadata.subtitle: Building and Managing Portfolios for Long-Term Wealth

content.title: Investment Portfolio Strategies
content.subtitle: Building and Managing Portfolios for Long-Term Wealth

content.label: The Power of Strategy
content.question:
Investor A: Perfectly picks individual stocks, 50/50 stock/bond allocation
Investor B: Uses simple index funds, 80/20 stock/bond allocation

Over 30 years, Investor B's simple allocation decision matters far more than Investor A's stock-picking genius.

Asset allocation explains over 90% of portfolio performance variance.

objectives.description: appropriate asset allocations based on time horizon, risk tolerance, and financial goals
different portfolio strategies including passive indexing and active management
the relationship between diversification, correlation, and portfolio risk

terms.definition: The percentage distribution of your portfolio across different asset classes (stocks, bonds, cash)
Periodically buying and selling to return portfolio to target allocation—typically done annually or when allocations drift significantly
terms.example: Selling stocks that grew to 75% to bring back to target 60%

terms.definition: Fund that automatically adjusts allocation to become more conservative as retirement approaches—'set it and forget it'
terms.example: Target Date 2050 Fund for someone retiring in 2050

terms.definition: Selling investments at a loss to offset capital gains taxes, then reinvesting in similar assets to maintain market exposure
terms.example: Sell losing stock, use loss to offset gains, buy similar stock

terms.definition: Strategic placement of investments in taxable vs. tax-advantaged accounts to minimize overall tax burden
terms.example: Put bonds in IRA, stocks in taxable account

terms.definition: How two investments move in relation to each other. Negative correlation (opposite movement) provides diversification benefits
terms.example: Stocks and bonds often have negative correlation

terms.definition: Investing fixed amounts at regular intervals regardless of market conditions—reduces impact of timing and volatility
Optimal portfolios offering highest expected return for each level of risk. Portfolios below the line are suboptimal
Tendency to overweight domestic investments—potentially missing international diversification benefits (U.S. = 60% of global market)
terms.example: Portfolio with 90% U.S. stocks despite global opportunities

leftColumn.title: Stocks
items: Higher long-term returns but significant volatility
Can gain 30% or lose 40% in a single year

rightColumn.title: Cash
items: Loses purchasing power to inflation over time

takeaways.title: Age 25: 85-90% Stocks / 10-15% Bonds
takeaways.description: 40-year horizon. Can weather volatility for higher growth

takeaways.title: Age 40: 70-75% Stocks / 25-30% Bonds
takeaways.description: 25-year horizon. Still growth-focused but adding stability

takeaways.title: Age 70: 40-45% Stocks / 55-60% Bonds
takeaways.description: Retirement. Preserving capital while maintaining growth

layoutData.title: Building a Diversified Portfolio
bulletPoints: Domestic Stocks: Large-cap, mid-cap, small-cap for different growth characteristics
International Stocks: Developed and emerging markets reduce home country bias
Bonds: Government, corporate, municipal for income and stability
Real Assets: REITs, commodities provide inflation hedge
Alternative Investments: May include hedge strategies for sophisticated investors
layoutData.keyPoint: True diversification means not all investments move together—reducing overall portfolio risk while maintaining return potential.

leftColumn.title: Passive (Index Funds)
items: Consistently outperforms 80-90% of active managers over 10+ years

rightColumn.title: Active Management
items: Professional managers try to beat the market
Most underperform index funds long-term after fees

layoutData.title: Why and When to Rebalance
paragraphs: Over time, winners grow and losers shrink, shifting your allocation away from targets.
Example: 60/40 stocks/bonds → After great stock year becomes 70/30 → Higher risk than intended!
Rebalancing forces you to 'sell high' (trim winners) and 'buy low' (add to losers).
bulletPoints: Calendar Method: Rebalance once or twice per year regardless of drift
Threshold Method: Rebalance when allocation drifts 5%+ from target
Tax Considerations: Use new contributions to rebalance without selling in taxable accounts

content.question: You're 22 years old and just started your first job with a 401(k). Your employer offers both index funds (0.05% fees) and actively managed funds (1.2% fees) that claim to beat the market. Which should you choose and why? How might your answer change at age 60?

scenario.name: Smart Tax Strategy
paragraphs: You bought Stock A for $10,000. It's now worth $8,000 (down $2,000).
You also have Stock B that gained $2,000 this year.
Without Tax-Loss Harvesting: Pay capital gains tax on $2,000 gain.
With Tax-Loss Harvesting: Sell Stock A to realize $2,000 loss. Use loss to offset $2,000 gain from Stock B. Buy similar stock to maintain market exposure. Result: $0 taxable gains this year!

outcomes.label: Tax Savings
outcomes.detail: Depending on your tax bracket, avoiding tax on $2,000 gain saves $300-$500

outcomes.label: Important Rules
outcomes.detail: Wash-sale rule: Can't buy identical security within 30 days. Must wait 31 days or buy similar (not identical) investment

infoCard.title: Taxable Accounts
items: Strategy: Maximize after-tax returns by placing right investments in right accounts

content.question: Many investors feel uncomfortable rebalancing—it means selling winners and buying losers, which feels wrong emotionally. How can understanding the mathematics of rebalancing help you overcome this psychological barrier? What discipline would you need to develop?

layoutData.title: Simple Yet Powerful Strategy
paragraphs: A three-fund portfolio provides global diversification with minimal complexity:
• Entire U.S. stock market (Large, mid, small cap)
layoutData.keyPoint: With just three low-cost index funds, you can build a globally diversified portfolio that most professional managers fail to beat.

takeaways.title: Asset allocation matters most
takeaways.description: Explains 90%+ of portfolio performance—more important than security selection

takeaways.title: Match allocation to time horizon
takeaways.description: Longer horizons allow higher stock allocation; shorter horizons need more bonds

takeaways.title: Passive beats active long-term
takeaways.description: 80-90% of active managers underperform index funds after fees

takeaways.title: Rebalance systematically
takeaways.description: Maintain target allocation by selling winners and buying losers

takeaways.title: Minimize costs and taxes
takeaways.description: Use low-cost index funds and strategic asset location

questions.question: Why does a 25-year-old typically have a higher stock allocation than a 60-year-old?
Explain how rebalancing forces you to 'buy low and sell high.'
What is the main advantage of passive index funds over actively managed funds?
How does tax-loss harvesting reduce your tax bill without changing your market exposure?

content.tagline: "Time in the market beats timing the market."
